kicker: ET LEVEL I // WHAT CONTROLS THE CURRENTS

beats.narration: Every eddy current has three working properties — strength, timing, and orientation — each one set jointly by your test system and the part.

payload.title: STRENGTH · TIMING · ORIENTATION
payload.sub: Three properties, set by system and part.

beats.narration: Strength starts with Faraday. More ampere-turns, higher frequency, and tighter coupling drive stronger currents; conductivity and permeability decide what the part does.
Timing comes from induction. Eddy currents alternate at your test frequency, oppose the change that made them — that's Lenz — and lag deeper down.
Orientation mirrors the coil. A surface probe drives loops parallel to the surface; an encircling coil drives them around. A flaw shows only where it cuts loops.

payload.heading: Orientation: the flaw must interrupt
payload.note: CRACK ACROSS LOOPS = SIGNAL · ALONG LOOPS = HIDDEN

beats.narration: And the currents are tiny — the part is a one-turn secondary at a fraction of a watt. Everything on screen is their field nudging coil impedance.

payload.sub: No heating, no direct measurement — read at the coil.
